title: Incident Postmortem
description: Use when writing a blameless postmortem for an outage, degradation, data incident, security incident, missed SLA, or operational near miss.

incident_classes.Availability outage: Requires error rate, latency, saturation, and dependency status; prevention focuses on redundancy, load shedding, rollout guardrails, and alert timing.
incident_classes.Performance degradation: Requires p95/p99, throughput, resource graphs, and slow traces; prevention focuses on capacity, budgets, backpressure, and autoscaling.
incident_classes.Data loss/corruption: Requires affected records, write path, restore point, and audit log; prevention focuses on write controls, validation, backups, and repair tooling.
incident_classes.Security/privacy: Requires exposed data, access path, actor, and containment time; prevention focuses on access control, detection, auditability, and disclosure workflow.
incident_classes.Failed release/change: Requires change diff, rollout timeline, canary signal, and rollback path; prevention focuses on release gates, canary coverage, flags, and ownership.
incident_classes.Dependency/vendor: Requires upstream status, retry/fallback behavior, and contracts; prevention focuses on circuit breakers, fallback modes, and dependency SLOs.
incident_classes.Near miss: Requires what almost failed, detection source, and avoided impact; prevention focuses on earlier detection, safer defaults, and runbook gaps.

cause_terms.Trigger: Immediate event that started the incident.
cause_terms.Root cause: System condition that made the trigger harmful.
cause_terms.Contributing factor: Condition that increased likelihood, duration, or impact.
cause_terms.Detection gap: Why the team did not know sooner or with enough detail.
cause_terms.Response gap: Why mitigation took longer or required ad hoc decisions.
cause_terms.Recovery factor: What helped reduce duration or impact.

instructions: Classify incident class and collect class-specific extra evidence before writing causes and actions.
Normalize incident facts: service, start/end time, detection source, severity, affected users/regions, customer/business impact, and data/security impact if any.
Build a chronological timeline from first signal through full recovery, marking uncertain timestamps as approximate.
Separate trigger, root cause, contributing factors, detection gaps, response gaps, and recovery factors using the defined cause terms; avoid person-blame language.
Analyze why existing controls did not prevent, detect, or limit the incident, including monitoring, tests, rollout controls, runbooks, ownership, dependency assumptions, and rollback capability.
Document what went well, what went poorly, and where responders lacked data or authority.
Define corrective actions with owner, due date, success metric, and verification method, separating immediate mitigations from durable prevention.
Include at least one action for prevention, one for detection/diagnosis, and one for response/recovery unless already covered with evidence.
Identify residual risks that remain after the proposed actions.

examples.input: Database CPU spike caused 45-min API outage.
examples.output: Summary: API requests failed for 45 minutes after a release increased database CPU saturation. Impact: affected API users during the incident window; quantify request failure rate and customer count if available. Root Cause: hot query path lacked an index required by the new access pattern. Contributing Factors: release canary did not include production-like query volume; alert fired after saturation. Actions: add the index, add query budget checks in CI, expand canary metrics to include DB CPU and slow-query rate, and verify with a replay or load test before closing.
